title: Dissolution of the Soviet Union
summary: On 26 December 1991 the Soviet of the Republics, the upper chamber of the USSR's Supreme Soviet, passed Declaration No. 142-N acknowledging that the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics had ceased to exist. The vote followed Mikhail Gorbachev's televised resignation the previous evening, the lowering of the red flag over the Kremlin, and the Belovezha Accords and Alma-Ata Protocol earlier that month. A superpower of some 293 million people and fifteen union republics, forged in revolution in 1917 and consecrated by victory over Nazi Germany in 1945, was quietly dismantled by its own leaders in a matter of weeks.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Soviet Union of 1985 was a superpower in slow crisis. Its planned economy had ceased to grow in real terms, its Afghan war was unwinnable, and its Eastern European satellites were held together by the threat of intervention. When the Politburo chose Mikhail Sergeyevich Gorbachev as General Secretary that March, they hoped for a younger, more energetic manager. Instead they got a reformer.

Gorbachev's twin programmes of perestroika (restructuring) and glasnost (openness) were meant to modernise the Soviet system. Enterprises were given a measure of autonomy, cooperatives were legalised, and press censorship was relaxed. Long-suppressed grievances, national, religious, environmental, historical, poured into public view. The 1986 Chernobyl disaster, initially concealed, damaged the state's credibility further. In 1988 Gorbachev renounced the Brezhnev doctrine that had licensed Soviet intervention in Eastern Europe. Within eighteen months the Berlin Wall had fallen (November 1989), the Warsaw Pact governments had collapsed, and Germany had reunified (October 1990).

The centrifugal forces then turned inward. Estonia declared sovereignty on 16 November 1988; Lithuania went further, declaring outright independence on 11 March 1990. In a 'parade of sovereignties' one republic after another asserted the primacy of its own laws over Moscow's. In Russia itself, Boris Yeltsin, a former Politburo member expelled by Gorbachev, was elected chair of the Russian Supreme Soviet in May 1990 and, in June 1991, became the first popularly elected president of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic. He held a democratic mandate Gorbachev did not.

Gorbachev tried to negotiate a looser 'Union of Sovereign States' through the so-called Novo-Ogaryovo process. A new treaty was scheduled for signing on 20 August 1991. It never happened. On 19 August a self-styled State Committee on the State of Emergency, led by Vice President Gennady Yanayev, KGB chief Vladimir Kryuchkov, and Defence Minister Dmitry Yazov, detained Gorbachev at his Crimean dacha and sent tanks into Moscow. Yeltsin climbed onto one of them outside the Russian White House and rallied resistance. Within three days the coup had collapsed; its leaders were arrested or, in one case, dead by suicide. Gorbachev returned to Moscow diminished; Yeltsin returned triumphant. The Communist Party of the Soviet Union was suspended, its property nationalised, and republic after republic declared full independence, Ukraine on 24 August, Belarus on 25 August, Moldova on 27 August, Azerbaijan on 30 August, Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan on 31 August. On 1 December Ukrainians voted overwhelmingly for independence in a referendum. The Union had, in effect, already ceased to function; only its formal burial remained.

image.caption: Boris Yeltsin addresses supporters outside the Russian White House on 22 August 1991, the day after the failed hardline coup.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On 8 December 1991 Boris Yeltsin of Russia, Leonid Kravchuk of Ukraine, and Stanislav Shushkevich, the parliamentary chairman of Belarus, met at a state hunting lodge at Viskuli in the Belovezha Forest of western Belarus. The three Slavic republics had been the core of the USSR; without them no reformed union was possible. After a day of talks the leaders signed the Belovezha Accords, whose opening line stated bluntly that 'the USSR, as a subject of international law and a geopolitical reality, is ceasing its existence' and established a new, looser Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) in its place. The document had been drafted overnight by aides working on typewriters; the leaders debated the wording line by line before signing at a small green baize table. Yeltsin telephoned US President George H. W. Bush from the lodge before informing Gorbachev, who learned of the death of his country from a subordinate and later described the phone call from Yeltsin as an insult.

The three legislatures ratified the accords with extraordinary speed. The Supreme Soviets of Belarus and Ukraine approved them on 10 December; the Russian Supreme Soviet on 12 December by a vote of 188 to 6. On 21 December, at a meeting in Alma-Ata (now Almaty), Kazakhstan, hosted by President Nursultan Nazarbayev, the leaders of eleven of the twelve remaining Soviet republics, all except Georgia, signed the Alma-Ata Protocol, formally adhering to the CIS and confirming that the Soviet Union had dissolved. The protocol allocated the USSR's UN Security Council seat and its strategic nuclear arsenal to Russia as successor state, and set out arrangements for shared debts and assets.

On Christmas Day, 25 December 1991, at 19:00 Moscow time, Gorbachev appeared on state television and announced his resignation as President of the Soviet Union, a country he acknowledged no longer existed. 'The old system collapsed before the new one had time to begin working,' he said, speaking for twelve minutes without interruption. He signed the resignation decree with a borrowed CNN pen, his own having failed. He then handed over the nuclear briefcase, the so-called Cheget, to Yeltsin's chief of staff after a last-minute dispute about protocol; Yeltsin himself, wounded by Gorbachev's parting speech, refused to attend in person. At 19:32 the red Soviet banner with its hammer and sickle was lowered from the flagpole above the Senate building of the Moscow Kremlin, and the white-blue-red tricolour of the Russian Federation was raised in its place. Foreign correspondents watched the change from Red Square in near silence.

The following day, 26 December 1991, the Soviet of the Republics, the only chamber of the Supreme Soviet that could still muster a quorum, formally passed Declaration No. 142-N recognising the extinction of the USSR. It released Soviet officials from their duties and dissolved the state's institutions. There was no ceremony, no crowd, no violence. Ministries were emptied, embassies re-flagged, and the Soviet delegation at the United Nations replaced by a Russian one on 31 December. The world's second superpower ended, in the historian Serhii Plokhy's phrase, 'not with a bang, but with a signature.'

image.caption: Kravchuk, Shushkevich and Yeltsin at the Viskuli hunting lodge, 8 December 1991, after signing the Belovezha Accords.
image.credit: U. Ivanov / RIA Novosti, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The fifteen successor states inherited nuclear weapons, borders drawn by Stalin, and economies wired for central planning. Under the 1994 Budapest Memorandum, Ukraine, Belarus, and Kazakhstan gave up the nuclear warheads on their soil to Russia in exchange for security assurances from Moscow, Washington, and London. Russia became the sole legal successor to the USSR at the United Nations and inherited its Security Council seat, embassies, and treaty obligations. Some 25 million ethnic Russians found themselves outside Russia; some 20 million non-Russians found themselves inside it. Millions eventually migrated, most notably ethnic Russians returning from Central Asia and the Caucasus.

Inside Russia, Yeltsin's government launched 'shock therapy' economic reforms under acting prime minister Yegor Gaidar in January 1992, freeing prices, ending subsidies, and, over the following years, privatising state assets through voucher schemes and the notorious 1995-1996 'loans-for-shares' auctions, which ultimately concentrated enormous wealth in the hands of a small group of 'oligarchs' including Boris Berezovsky, Vladimir Potanin, Mikhail Khodorkovsky, and Roman Abramovich. Output fell by roughly forty per cent between 1991 and 1998; industrial production halved; male life expectancy dropped from 63 years in 1990 to under 58 by 1994; the 1998 rouble crisis wiped out household savings a second time. In several other former republics the transition was harder still. Armed conflict broke out in Nagorno-Karabakh between Armenia and Azerbaijan, in Transnistria in Moldova, in Abkhazia and South Ossetia in Georgia, in Tajikistan (whose civil war of 1992-1997 killed some 50,000), and, from December 1994, in Chechnya inside Russia itself, opening two brutal wars over the following decade.

Geopolitically the dissolution ended the Cold War definitively. The Warsaw Pact had already been dissolved in July 1991; Comecon, the Soviet-led economic bloc, went the same way. NATO expanded eastward from 1999, taking in the former Warsaw Pact states of the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Poland, and, in 2004, seven more countries including the three Baltic states. The United States entered what commentators would call a 'unipolar moment'; Francis Fukuyama's 'end of history' thesis appeared, briefly, vindicated. In the former Soviet space the trajectories diverged sharply: the Baltic states joined the European Union and NATO in 2004; Central Asian republics settled into varieties of authoritarian rule under long-serving strongmen; Ukraine and Georgia oscillated between Moscow and the West through the 'colour revolutions' of 2003-2005 and beyond.

For many Russians the memory of 1991 became bitter. Gorbachev, celebrated abroad as a peacemaker who had won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1990, was widely disdained at home for presiding over national humiliation; he received a humiliating 0.5 per cent of the vote in his 1996 presidential bid. Vladimir Putin, who succeeded Yeltsin as president on the last day of 1999, would famously describe the collapse of the Soviet Union in 2005 as 'the greatest geopolitical catastrophe' of the twentieth century, a phrase that would echo through the Russian invasions of Georgia in 2008 and Ukraine in 2014 and 2022.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The dissolution of the Soviet Union is one of the rare cases in modern history of a great power dismantling itself largely without war. For a brief window in the early 1990s it seemed to vindicate a triumphant liberal narrative: Francis Fukuyama's essay 'The End of History' had appeared in 1989, and its argument, that liberal democracy might be the final form of human government, seemed borne out. That optimism has not aged evenly. Democracy consolidated in some post-Soviet states, receded in others, and never took root in a third group. By the mid-2020s Freedom House rated only the three Baltic states as fully 'free'; most of the rest ranged from partly free to consolidated authoritarian.

The end of the USSR also reshaped the global map. Fifteen new states joined the United Nations, three of them, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, restoring an independence they had lost in 1940 under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact. Ukraine, with more than fifty million people at independence, became the largest European country outside Russia. Central Asia rejoined a wider Islamic world it had been partly severed from since the nineteenth century; the region's borders, drawn by Stalin, contained the seeds of the frozen conflicts and water disputes that would flare in subsequent decades. Georgia, Armenia, and Azerbaijan reclaimed distinct national trajectories, though not without new borders drawn in blood.

Economically, the dissolution triggered one of the deepest peacetime contractions of the twentieth century, and only the resource-rich states, Russia most obviously, recovered quickly when commodity prices rose in the 2000s. The privatisation of Soviet-era industry created oligarch classes whose fortunes and political alignments still shape the region. Post-Soviet demography was catastrophic: Russia's population fell from 148 million in 1991 to 142 million by 2010; deaths from alcoholism, suicide, and cardiovascular disease surged among middle-aged men.

Historians continue to argue about causation. Some emphasise long-term economic stagnation and imperial overreach; others credit Gorbachev's reforms, or his failure to control them; a third school points to national movements in Ukraine, the Baltics, and the Caucasus that made a reformed union impossible. A fourth locates the decisive moment in the failed August 1991 coup, which discredited the central institutions beyond recovery. What is beyond dispute is that on 26 December 1991 a state that had defined the twentieth century, industrial, ideological, nuclear, victorious over Nazism, defeated in Afghanistan, was voted out of existence by its own representatives, and a new and unfinished chapter of Eurasian history began, one whose closing shape is still being contested on the battlefields of eastern Ukraine three decades later.

worldContext.text: The United States under President George H. W. Bush was celebrating its victory in the Gulf War, which had ended with the liberation of Kuwait in February 1991, and adjusting to its status as the sole remaining superpower. A recession sapped the president's popularity even as his foreign policy soared. In Canada, Brian Mulroney's government pushed forward the North American Free Trade Agreement, signed in 1992. In Latin America, El Salvador's civil war ended with the Chapultepec Peace Accords in January 1992, and Argentina and Chile had recently completed transitions from military rule.

China was still recovering from the international isolation that had followed the Tiananmen Square crackdown of June 1989. Deng Xiaoping was preparing his celebrated 'southern tour' of January 1992, which would restart market reforms and open the coastal special economic zones to a fresh wave of foreign investment. Japan's asset-price bubble had burst in 1990, beginning the 'lost decade' of stagnation. South Korea and Taiwan continued their democratic consolidation; both were admitted to the United Nations, along with the two Koreas, in September 1991.

In South Africa, formal negotiations to end apartheid had begun with the Convention for a Democratic South Africa (CODESA) on 20 December 1991, six days before the Soviet dissolution. Nelson Mandela, freed the previous year, and President F. W. de Klerk were charting a path to majority rule. Elsewhere the end of Cold War patronage destabilised client regimes: Somalia collapsed into civil war after the fall of Siad Barre in January 1991; Ethiopia's Mengistu regime fell that May; Angola and Mozambique moved toward fragile peace negotiations.

The Middle East was reshaped by the Gulf War coalition's victory over Iraq. Saddam Hussein remained in power in Baghdad under sweeping UN sanctions and no-fly zones. The Madrid Peace Conference of October-November 1991 had, for the first time, brought Israel and its Arab neighbours, including a Palestinian delegation, to the same table, opening a path that would lead to the Oslo Accords of 1993. In Algeria the ruling FLN, faced with an imminent Islamist election victory, cancelled the second round in January 1992, triggering a decade-long civil war.

Australia in December 1991 was in the midst of a political upheaval: Paul Keating deposed Bob Hawke as leader of the Labor Party on 19 December and became prime minister the following day, promising a more assertively republican, Asia-focused Australia. New Zealand under Jim Bolger's National government was continuing the free-market restructuring begun in the 1980s. Across the Pacific islands, the end of Cold War strategic competition prompted a modest French drawdown and renewed debate over nuclear testing at Mururoa Atoll.

figures.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
figures.role: Last General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union and last President of the USSR; resigned 25 December 1991
figures.credit: Vladimir Vyatkin / RIA Novosti, CC BY-SA 3.0

figures.name: Boris Yeltsin
figures.role: First elected President of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic; co-signer of the Belovezha Accords
figures.credit: Presidential Press and Information Office (Kremlin.ru), CC BY 4.0

figures.name: Leonid Kravchuk
figures.role: First President of independent Ukraine; co-signer of the Belovezha Accords

figures.name: Stanislav Shushkevich
figures.role: Chairman of the Supreme Council of Belarus; hosted the Belovezha meeting

figures.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
figures.role: President of Kazakhstan; host of the Alma-Ata meeting of 21 December 1991 that formalised the Commonwealth of Independent States

images.caption: Leonid Kravchuk of Ukraine, Stanislav Shushkevich of Belarus, and Boris Yeltsin of Russia at the Viskuli hunting lodge in the Belovezha Forest, 8 December 1991, after signing the accords that dissolved the USSR.
images.credit: U. Ivanov / RIA Novosti, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Boris Yeltsin addresses supporters outside the Russian White House in Moscow, 22 August 1991, the day after the failed hardline coup against Gorbachev.

sources.title: Mikhail Gorbachev, the last Soviet leader, dies at 91
The Last Empire: The Final Days of the Soviet Union
